skill.name: sql-expert
skill.description: SQL guidance for writing queries, optimizing execution plans, designing schemas, creating indexes, and planning safer migrations across PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and SQL Server.
skill.summary: Write, optimize, and review SQL queries, schemas, indexes, and migrations.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity weak-cryptography and reconnaissance findings appear to be false positives from SQL terminology, examples, and markdown code fences. The real publish risk is the included Python helper, which can connect to databases and execute caller-supplied SQL. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Database Helper Executes Supplied SQL
medium_findings.description: The helper opens SQLAlchemy database connections and executes SQL strings supplied by the caller. This is expected for a SQL utility, but running it against privileged databases can read or modify real data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly creates database engines and passes query strings into SQLAlchemy text execution. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but impact depends on the target database privileges.

medium_findings.title: Query Builder Interpolates SQL Identifiers and Clauses
medium_findings.description: The query builder directly interpolates table names, column lists, joins, WHERE clauses, GROUP BY clauses, and ORDER BY clauses. This can become SQL injection if callers pass untrusted input into those structural fields.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: String interpolation is visible in the query builder, while only values are returned separately as parameters. This is a common utility pattern, but unsafe if identifiers or clauses are user-controlled.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are markdown code fences and inline filename references, not executable command invocations. The installation commands shown are user-facing dependency instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are fenced markdown snippets or inline documentation. No shell execution API or runtime command dispatch appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are SQL Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits map to SQL and documentation terms such as DEFAULT, ORDER BY, ranking, constraints, and hash join. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic use in the sampled flagged files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines show SQL examples and performance vocabulary rather than cryptographic algorithms. A targeted check found no weak cryptographic algorithm names in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Findings Are Database Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance hits are SQL examples and database-performance guidance, such as scans, joins, keys, and query pitfalls. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment collection, or system discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are database documentation and SQL queries. They do not access operating system APIs or collect system information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime SQL Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: SQL strings are executed through SQLAlchemy text calls against caller-provided database connections.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution path is explicit. The security impact depends on database privileges and caller input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated SQL Clause Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: SQL identifiers and structural clauses are composed with string interpolation instead of identifier validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The builder joins caller-provided strings into SQL. It is safe only when callers control or validate those strings.

content.value_statement: Database work often fails because queries are slow, schemas drift, or migrations are risky. This skill gives structured SQL patterns, reviews, and helper workflows for safer database development.
actual_capabilities: Drafts SELECT queries with joins, CTEs, subqueries, aggregates, and window functions.
Explains EXPLAIN plans and recommends practical index improvements.
Guides schema design with normalization, constraints, keys, and data types.
Provides safe migration patterns with rollback and zero-downtime considerations.
Includes a Python helper for SQLAlchemy connections, introspection, timing, and query building.
Covers PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and SQL Server syntax considerations.
limitations: It does not verify performance against your production data without database access.
The helper script can execute SQL and should only run against approved databases.
Generated SQL still needs review for local naming, permissions, and data retention rules.
Database-specific behavior may require adjustment for extensions, versions, and engine settings.

use_cases.title: Tune a Slow Report Query
use_cases.description: Review a slow analytics query, explain likely bottlenecks, and propose indexes or query rewrites.

use_cases.title: Design a New Feature Schema
use_cases.description: Turn product requirements into normalized tables, constraints, relationships, and migration steps.

use_cases.title: Review a Risky Migration
use_cases.description: Check a schema migration for locking, rollback strategy, batching, and compatibility concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Explain This Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this SQL query in plain language. Identify each join, filter, aggregate, and sort step. Then list any obvious risks.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Query Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SQL query and table summary. Suggest index changes, query rewrites, and EXPLAIN checks before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Database Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a relational schema for these requirements. Include tables, keys, constraints, indexes, and migration notes for PostgreSQL.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this migration plan for production risk. Focus on locks, backfills, rollback, data validation, and zero-downtime deployment.

output_examples.input: A slow customer report query with joins across orders, order items, and products.
output: Identifies likely scan and join bottlenecks.
Recommends targeted indexes for join keys and date filters.
Suggests validating changes with an execution plan before rollout.

output_examples.input: Requirements for accounts, invoices, payments, and refunds.
output: Proposes normalized tables with primary and foreign keys.
Adds constraints for money amounts, statuses, and timestamps.
Calls out migration order and rollback considerations.

output_examples.input: A migration that adds a required column to a large table.
output: Recommends staged deployment with nullable add, batched updates, then constraint enforcement.
Includes validation checks for missing values.

best_practices: Provide schema, row counts, indexes, and database engine version when asking for optimization help.
Use parameterized values and validate SQL identifiers before running generated helper queries.
Test migrations on realistic data and keep a rollback plan for every production change.
anti_patterns: Do not run helper-generated SQL against production before review and backup planning.
Do not pass user-controlled table names, column names, or clauses into the query builder.
Do not treat generic index suggestions as final without checking actual execution plans.

faq.question: Which databases does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and SQL Server patterns, with strongest detail for common relational SQL workflows.

faq.question: Can it execute SQL automatically?
faq.answer: The skill includes a Python helper that can execute SQL when a user runs it with database credentials.

faq.question: Is it safe for production databases?
faq.answer: Use it with production only after review, backups, least-privilege credentials, and change-management approval.

faq.question: Can it improve existing queries?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the query, schema, indexes, row counts, and execution plan for more accurate recommendations.

faq.question: Does it create migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides migration patterns and review guidance, including rollback, batching, and zero-downtime concerns.

faq.question: Does it replace a database administrator?
faq.answer: No. It supports analysis and drafting, but critical schema and production changes need expert review.
